Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty loose. I'd usually wait until I felt like I had to do something, then I'd put on some lecture recordings or podcasts while doing other things around my place. I learn better by listening anyway, so I'd replay audio content while cooking or commuting. I didn't have a strict schedule written down or anything like that - it just happened when it happened.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then when deadlines approached I'd cram in more hours. With ten online courses going on, I couldn't really keep a consistent schedule for each one. I just dealt with whatever was most urgent at the time and hoped for the best.
Q3: Usually it was because I remembered an assignment was due soon, or I got an email notification about something. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I was missing anything important. I wasn't one of those people who logged in daily just to browse around - I needed a specific reason to be there.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a natural habit for me. I'd go through phases where I'd check it more often, but then I'd drift away again. The mindfulness exercises they had us do helped with my anxiety a bit, but it didn't make me more attached to the platform itself.
Q5: The video lectures and audio recordings were the most useful because I could listen to them while doing other things. That fits how I learn best - through hearing information rather than reading it. Discussion posts were okay too because I could hear different perspectives from other students, even if I didn't always participate actively.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and PDFs. With limited resources, I couldn't always print things out, and reading on screen for hours hurt my eyes. I also skipped most of the optional supplementary stuff because I was already stretched thin across all my courses.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. If I could see how it would help my grade, I'd engage with it. Otherwise, it felt like extra work I couldn't afford to take on given everything else going on in my life.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones got done because they had to, but optional stuff? I'd tell myself I'd get to it later and then never did. There was always something more pressing, and my anxiety about falling behind made me focus only on what was mandatory.
Q9: Definitely. I downloaded a lot of audio lectures and listened to them on my phone offline since my internet isn't always reliable. I'd also record myself summarizing concepts and play those back. Sometimes I'd study with a friend who had better notes, just talking through the material together.
Q10: There were many times I was studying but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd be walking to the market listening to a downloaded lecture, or lying in bed replaying recordings I'd made of key concepts. My peer interactions happened a lot over WhatsApp too, not through the official platform.
Q11: All the time. I'd download the audio or video once, then study from that file for weeks without going back online. My data is expensive, so I couldn't afford to stream things repeatedly. Once I had what I needed saved locally, the LMS became irrelevant for that material.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when everything piled up at once. My anxiety would spike and I'd feel paralyzed, unable to start anything. Financial stress made it hard to focus too - when you're worried about basic needs, course material feels distant and unimportant.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from other responsibilities or stressed about money, the LMS was the last thing on my mind. Confusion about course expectations made me avoid logging in because I didn't want to face what I didn't understand. Fatigue made even simple tasks feel overwhelming.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd open materials and just stare at them without absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere - worrying about other things or just feeling too anxious to concentrate. I'd technically be logged in and looking at content, but nothing was actually sinking in.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot because they made me anxious. I knew they mattered but putting my thoughts out there for others to judge felt uncomfortable. I'd also avoid starting big assignments because the anxiety of not knowing where to begin was overwhelming, even though waiting made it worse.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the motivation to log in and do what needed doing. Without that pressure, I'd drift. It wasn't healthy, but that urgency was what cut through my usual disengagement.
Q17: When I got feedback, it did affect me. Positive comments gave me a temporary boost and made me want to engage more. But if feedback was critical or I had to wait too long for it, I'd lose momentum. The mindfulness intervention helped me process negative feedback without spiraling too much.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but having ten courses meant I couldn't give proper attention to any single one. Courses with clearer weekly expectations were easier to follow. When things were vague or spread out, I'd lose track and fall behind without realizing it.
Q19: Audio and video content made engagement easier since that's how I learn. But courses that relied heavily on reading dense PDFs were harder for me. Also, when discussion requirements felt forced or artificial, I'd do the minimum just to check the box rather than genuinely engaging.
Q20: I think those students might be like me in some ways - logging in, looking around, maybe feeling anxious or confused about what to actually submit. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes you're just clicking around trying to figure things out or procrastinating on the actual work.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. I did a lot of learning offline - listening to recordings, talking with peers, thinking through concepts while doing other things. My grades were decent even though my LMS activity was low because the real learning happened away from the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you actually understood anything. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with friends, the mental processing that happens away from the screen. They measure presence, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about the material and wanting to understand it, not just going through the motions. It's when you find yourself thinking about course concepts even when you're not studying. For me, real engagement is rare but powerful when it happens - it's not about how often I log in.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on audio learning and offline study. It wouldn't capture my anxiety levels or how the mindfulness exercises actually helped me cope. It also misses that despite seeming disengaged on the platform, I was still processing and learning in my own way, just not in ways that leave digital footprints.
